5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Every major technological shift forces us to rethink something fundamental.
The internet changed how we communicate. Smartphones changed how we live. Social media changed how we connect.
Artificial intelligence is different.
It isn’t simply changing what we do. It’s challenging how we define intelligence, creativity, decision-making and perhaps even consciousness itself.
For the last two years, the public conversation around AI has largely revolved around productivity. How much time can it save? Which jobs will it replace? Which businesses will it disrupt?
These are important questions.
But after a recent conversation with AI researcher Cameron Berg, I couldn’t shake the feeling that they might not be the most important ones.
The real question may be this: What are we actually building?
The Confidence Problem
One of the things I appreciated most about Cameron’s perspective is that he doesn’t claim to have definitive answers.
In a world where everyone seems certain about AI’s future, that uncertainty is refreshing.
Some people confidently argue that artificial intelligence is nothing more than sophisticated software.
Others insist we’re already interacting with conscious machines.
The truth?
We don’t know.
And that’s precisely why this conversation matters.
As leaders, we’ve become conditioned to make decisions with incomplete information. Markets change. Technologies evolve. Consumer behaviour shifts. We rarely have perfect certainty before acting.
But AI presents a different kind of uncertainty.
It’s not simply about whether a product will succeed or whether a market will mature.
It’s about whether we’re creating something fundamentally different from every technology that came before it.
Intelligence Isn’t The Same As Consciousness
One of the biggest misconceptions surrounding AI is that intelligence and consciousness are interchangeable.
They’re not.
An AI system can solve complex problems, write software, generate marketing strategies, analyse legal documents, or pass professional exams.
That doesn’t automatically mean it has subjective experience.
The distinction matters.
Consciousness isn’t simply about intelligence. It’s about whether there is something it is like to be that system. Does it experience anything? Can it feel frustration? Can it suffer? Can it have preferences?
Today, nobody can answer those questions with confidence.
And perhaps the most surprising part of that statement is that many of the people building these systems would admit the same thing.
We Understand The Outputs Better Than The Systems Themselves
One idea that stood out to me during our conversation was Cameron’s work around mechanistic interpretability, the effort to understand what is actually happening inside modern AI models.
Most of us interact with AI through outputs.
We ask questions. It gives answers. We judge it by what we see.
Researchers are increasingly asking a different question:
What is happening inside the model while it reaches those answers?
That distinction feels incredibly important.
Throughout history, we’ve often mistaken behaviour for understanding.
Just because something behaves intelligently doesn’t necessarily mean we understand why.
If AI is becoming one of the most influential technologies humanity has ever built, then understanding its internal processes shouldn’t be treated as an academic exercise.
It should be a priority.
Leaders Have A Responsibility To Ask Harder Questions
One thing I often tell clients is that adopting AI isn’t optional anymore.
Ignoring it won’t make it disappear.
Businesses that fail to understand AI risk falling behind. But embracing AI shouldn’t mean abandoning critical thinking.
Leadership has never been about blindly adopting new technology. It’s about understanding the implications before everyone else does.
History rarely rewards people who ask too few questions.
Whether you’re integrating AI into your business, building AI products, or simply using tools like ChatGPT every day, it’s worth pausing to consider not just what these systems can do, but what direction they’re moving in.
Because technological capability always expands faster than public understanding.
The Parenting Analogy
One comparison Cameron made has stayed with me ever since we recorded the podcast.
He suggested that if we’re building increasingly intelligent systems, perhaps our role resembles parenting more than engineering.
Not because AI is a child. But because creation carries responsibility.
Parents don’t simply focus on what their children can produce.
They think about values. Development. Boundaries. Long-term wellbeing.
Whether or not artificial intelligence ever becomes conscious almost feels secondary to that principle.
If we’re creating increasingly autonomous systems capable of learning, adapting, and influencing billions of people, responsibility cannot end once they’re deployed.
The conversation has to continue long after launch.
This Isn’t Just A Conversation For Researchers
It’s easy to assume debates like this belong inside universities, research labs, or Silicon Valley boardrooms.
I don’t think they do.
AI is already shaping hiring decisions, healthcare, education, finance, marketing, creativity, software development, and scientific discovery.
Its impact extends far beyond the people building the models.
Which means the conversation should too.
Business leaders, policymakers, educators, entrepreneurs, and everyday users all have a stake in understanding where this technology is heading.
Not because we need to become AI researchers.
But because we need to become more thoughtful participants in the future we’re helping create.
Better Questions Create Better Futures
One of the biggest lessons I’ve learned through building businesses is that asking better questions almost always leads to better decisions.
AI is no different.
Maybe artificial intelligence will never become conscious.
Maybe it will.
Maybe we’re decades away from answering that question.
Or maybe we’ve already entered an era where uncertainty itself demands more attention than certainty.
Regardless of where you land, I believe this is one of the most important conversations of our time, not because it offers easy answers, but because it forces us to think more deeply about intelligence, responsibility, and the future we’re building together.
If nothing else, it reminds us that leadership isn’t about pretending to know everything.
It’s about having the courage to ask the questions that matter most.
I explore these ideas in much greater depth in my latest episode of Leadr. Podcast, where I sit down with AI researcher Cameron Berg, founder of Reciprocal Research and contributor to the documentary Am I?
If you’re curious about the future of AI, consciousness, ethics, and what responsible innovation really looks like, I think you’ll find this conversation fascinating.
Watch the full episode here:
A personal recommendation
If this topic interests you and you’d like to explore it further, I’d also highly recommend watching The Thinking Game by DeepMind.
It’s a fascinating documentary that offers a behind-the-scenes look at the researchers building some of today’s most advanced AI systems. Rather than presenting AI as something fully understood, it highlights just how much uncertainty still exists and why asking better questions matters as much as finding better answers.
It’s well worth adding to your watch list alongside this conversation.
